title: Good Grief

jokes.text: Michael was adjusting to his new position as vice president, which meant doing the work of the president, his brother Gob.
jokes.setup: Michael is described as 'adjusting to his new position as vice president.'
jokes.punchline: The adjustment is that he does all the actual work while Gob, the incompetent president, does nothing.
jokes.explanation: Efficient narrator joke that establishes the absurdity of the Bluth family power structure. Works because it's completely in character for Gob to hold a title and do zero work. Good economy, delivered with deadpan narrator voice.

jokes.text: So, did you see the new Poof? / His name's Gary, and we don't need any more lawsuits.
jokes.setup: Gob asks Michael if he's seen 'the new Poof,' which sounds like he's referring to a gay employee named Gary.
jokes.punchline: Michael assumes Gob is referring to Gary, implying Gob has been making inappropriate remarks about Gary's sexuality, leading to lawsuit threats — before Gob clarifies 'Poof' is a magician's magazine.
jokes.explanation: A masterclass in layered misdirection. 'Poof' works simultaneously as British slang for gay person AND the name of a real magicians' magazine. The joke is tripled: the double meaning of 'Poof,' the revelation that Gary is gay, and Michael's completely plausible assumption that Gob has been harassing Gary. Character-perfect — Gob would absolutely say these things to Gary and not see the problem. One of the best-constructed jokes of the episode.

jokes.text: Gob says 'You've got a nice mouth. I'd kill for that ass.' then clarifies he was talking about his office chair.
jokes.setup: Gob fears Gary will think he was coming on to him, then delivers what sounds like a sexually charged compliment.
jokes.punchline: He was talking about his office chair — which has been giving out when he leans back.
jokes.explanation: Very strong structure: Gob worries about being misread as coming on to Gary, then immediately demonstrates exactly the kind of thing that could be misread, before pulling back to reveal it was about a chair. The two-stage misdirection is elegant. 'I'd kill for that ass' is highly quotable. Perfect Gob obliviousness — he has no self-awareness about how his words land.

jokes.text: Gob claims he had Tony Wonder's idea 10 months ago — but his version was boiling himself alive into a chowder to entertain and feed firemen.
jokes.setup: Gob insists he had the idea of baking himself into food to entertain troops before Tony Wonder did.
jokes.punchline: His version involved boiling himself alive into a chowder and being ladled out of a giant cauldron — which is both more dangerous and completely insane.
jokes.explanation: The joke lands on multiple levels: Gob's idea is both derivative (he claims originality) AND far more idiotic and dangerous. 'Boil myself alive into a chowder' is perfectly escalated absurdism. 'That's pretty close' from Michael is the ideal straight-man response. High character integration — only Gob would believe this was both original AND a good idea.

jokes.text: Gob says 'I'm halfway to getting out of this office and into a steaming bowl of soup. Everybody wins.'
jokes.setup: Michael summarizes the plan: Dad goes to jail, Michael gets cleared, becomes president again.
jokes.punchline: Gob's contribution to 'everybody wins' is that he's one step closer to his insane soup illusion.
jokes.explanation: Callback to the chowder illusion that deepens the absurdity. Gob treating a magician's bowl-of-soup stunt as a serious personal goal, equal in weight to Michael's business and legal concerns, is perfectly in character. Economy is strong.

jokes.text: Lucille tells Oscar: 'Close it. You look like the window of a butcher shop.'
jokes.setup: Oscar is present, presumably with his shirt open or in some disheveled state.
jokes.punchline: Lucille's cruel, casually delivered insult comparing him to a butcher's window display.
jokes.explanation: Sharp, original insult with unusual imagery. 'Window of a butcher shop' is not a stock phrase — it's an oddly specific and vivid image that suggests Oscar's exposed body looks like hanging meat. Very Lucille: effortlessly cruel, no elaboration needed. Extremely economical.

jokes.text: George Michael has closed the banana stand to spend time with Ann; Michael tells him to keep his phone on and calls him 'pal.'
jokes.setup: Michael finds the banana stand closed during work hours.
jokes.punchline: Michael's passive-aggressive 'Sure. No problem. Just keep the phone on. Okay, pal?' is a masterclass in dad-disapproval-through-false-acceptance.
jokes.explanation: Solid character comedy about Michael's barely-disguised disapproval of Ann. The 'pal' is a nice touch of strained cheerfulness. Functional but not exceptional.

jokes.text: Michael: 'Oh, she's still goin'? Listen, I'll talk to you later. I'm very excited.'
jokes.setup: George Michael says he's still talking with Ann.
jokes.punchline: Michael's dismissive 'Oh, she's still goin'?' treats Ann as an inconvenience/endurance event, then pivots to his own excitement.
jokes.explanation: Michael's thinly veiled contempt for Ann in casual phrasing. 'She's still goin'?' is a funny framing — like Ann is a nuisance appliance running past its useful life. Good character integration, functional joke.

jokes.text: George Michael, immediately after the phone call is cut, says 'I think we should break up' — followed by a coughing sob from Ann.
jokes.setup: The scene has been building romantic tension around Ann's complaint about interruptions.
jokes.punchline: The immediate cut back reveals George Michael breaking up with Ann — the phone call apparently galvanized a decision, and Ann's response is a pathetic 'coughing sob.'
jokes.explanation: The timing is sharp — we've barely seen the Ann/George Michael relationship and it collapses almost in the background. 'Coughing sob' in script form implies a spectacularly undignified breakup cry from Ann, which is funny and consistent with the show's treatment of her as a character without glamour.

jokes.text: Ice the bounty hunter has 'always bounty-hunted to support his first love — party planning.'
jokes.setup: Ice is introduced as a tough bounty hunter.
jokes.punchline: The narrator reveals his real passion is party planning, upending the tough-guy archetype.
jokes.explanation: Classic subversion of tough-guy archetype. The matter-of-fact narrator delivery makes it land better. Sets up jokes throughout the rest of the episode about Ice's dual nature.

jokes.text: Lindsay asks Ice if he'd be interested in 'catering an affair with me.' Ice responds: 'Well, when you get a full guest list, let me know.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay is trying to flirt/proposition Ice.
jokes.punchline: Her flirtatious phrasing ('catering an affair with me') is heard by Ice literally as a catering request; his professional response deflates the seduction.
jokes.explanation: Elegant double-meaning wordplay. 'Affair' does the heavy lifting — it means both a romantic affair and a social event to be catered. Ice's literal response is perfectly deadpan and sets up the entire episode's running joke about him as caterer/bounty hunter. The line works on both readings simultaneously.

jokes.text: Tobias admits he's surprised Lindsay is 'going after somebody so similar to my own type' and that 'we all do expose our inner desires.'
jokes.setup: Tobias is commenting on Lindsay flirting with Ice.
jokes.punchline: His description of Ice as 'similar to my own type' is an accidental confession of his latent homosexuality, which he immediately and obliviously denies.
jokes.explanation: Classic Tobias obliviousness about his sexuality. The show's running joke that Tobias is in denial about being gay is executed here through his own words. George Michael's 'I think you just did' / 'No, I didn't' exchange is the perfect straight-man beat. High character integration — only Tobias could land this.

jokes.text: George Michael's 'I think you just did' response to Tobias's accidental confession.
jokes.setup: Tobias says 'we all do expose our inner desires, don't we?'
jokes.punchline: George Michael's understated 'I think you just did' — pointing out the self-exposure Tobias doesn't recognize.
jokes.explanation: Perfect economy — four words do all the work. George Michael as deadpan observer of adult absurdity is one of the show's great straight-man roles. Works as the payoff beat to Tobias's line.

jokes.text: George Michael asks Barry if he can 'divorce' his parents; Barry says it's called emancipation but he'd need to prove an 'unstable environment' — then notes both parents are trying to have affairs.
jokes.setup: George Michael, frustrated by his parents' behavior, asks if he can divorce them.
jokes.punchline: Barry confirms it's possible (emancipation) and the criteria are essentially already being met — both parents trying to have affairs — and then cheerfully advises they get it done.
jokes.explanation: Works because Barry immediately pivots from family attorney to enabler of a child suing his parents. The deadpan 'if they do, I could have you out of this house' is funny because it's presented as practical legal advice. George Michael's 'Sweet!' response is the ideal punchline button.

jokes.text: George Michael responds 'Sweet!' to learning he could be emancipated and on the street in a month.
jokes.setup: Barry lays out that successful affairs by both parents could lead to emancipation.
jokes.punchline: George Michael's enthusiastic 'Sweet!' — treating the prospect of being a homeless teenager as a win.
jokes.explanation: One-word punchline that earns its laugh through George Michael's desperate situation being so bad that homelessness sounds appealing. Good economy, solid character moment.

jokes.text: Ice's presentation includes 'a political cartoon from a leading Mexican publication' as evidence of George Sr.'s death.
jokes.setup: Ice presents what should be hard evidence of George Sr.'s death: dental records and a death certificate.
jokes.punchline: He also includes a political cartoon as a third piece of evidence, presented with equal gravity.
jokes.explanation: The joke is in the unearned equivalence: dental records, death certificate, and... a political cartoon. Ice's professional confidence while presenting a cartoon as forensic evidence is wonderfully absurd. 'From a leading Mexican publication' adds extra pomposity.

jokes.text: Confusion about who killed whom: 'I'm sorry — he killed a guard?' / 'No, the guard killed him.'
jokes.setup: Ice gives his presentation about George Sr.'s death, saying 'Until he was killed by this guard.'
jokes.punchline: Michael assumes George Sr. killed a guard; Ice clarifies the guard killed him — a small but funny beat about the family's reflexive assumption of George Sr.'s guilt.
jokes.explanation: Quick, efficient beat revealing that Michael's default assumption about his father is that he murders people. Good character insight in very few words. Not especially memorable but functional.

jokes.text: Ice interrupts the revelation of George Sr.'s death to remind the family he's available for catering: 'I'm a caterer — if you do have any family events — weddings, wakes —'
jokes.setup: The family is reeling from the news that George Sr. is dead.
jokes.punchline: Ice immediately pivots to a catering pitch — including 'wakes' — while the family absorbs devastating news.
jokes.explanation: Ice's pitch to cater the wake of a man whose death he just announced is brilliantly timed and character-consistent. 'Wakes' is the perfect word to include — it's simultaneously appropriate and horrifying. The deadpan professionalism while the family is in shock is a great comedic contrast.

jokes.text: Just checking. [Pinches Lucille after she cries 'Is it true? Is he really gone?']
jokes.setup: Lucille appears emotionally overwhelmed, asking if it's true George Sr. is gone.
jokes.punchline: Someone pinches her (implied by 'Ow') — 'just checking' — suggesting even the family doubts the sincerity of her grief.
jokes.explanation: The 'Ow / Just checking' exchange is a quick, effective bit — the pinch to test if she's dreaming subverts the emotional moment with dark comedy. Simple but well-timed.

jokes.text: Family members sentimentally recall George Sr.'s favorite toothpaste: 'It was that Glisten. He swore by that Glisten. I can still hear him now: 'Who left the cap off my [bleeps] Glisten?'
jokes.setup: Family members wax nostalgic over George Sr.'s 'perfect teeth.'
jokes.punchline: The beloved memory is of George Sr. angrily cursing about his toothpaste cap — the bleeps suggesting a profane tirade over something trivial.
jokes.explanation: Excellent use of the bleep: we get the joke without hearing the words — George Sr.'s most vivid legacy is an obscene rant about toothpaste. Fondly remembering someone's worst behavior as their defining characteristic is a sharp bit of dark comedy. Also plants Glisten as a setup for a later payoff.

jokes.text: Lucille's theatrical grief spiral: 'The will. We have to get the will... Is it in your car, Barry? I'm gonna throw up! Check the car, Barry. I don't know what I'm saying!'
jokes.setup: Lucille is supposedly grieving, but her grief keeps circling back to the will.
jokes.punchline: The escalating non-sequitur spiral — grief, will, car, vomit, will again — reveals she's performing grief while actually panicking about the estate.
jokes.explanation: The repetition of 'I don't know what I'm saying' while saying exactly what she means is perfectly constructed Lucille comedy. Her grief is entirely mercenary, and the manic spiral performance is very funny. 'Check the car, Barry' as a grief response is wonderfully specific.

jokes.text: Barry: 'The will is at my office, next to the hot plate with the frayed wires.' — followed by narrator: 'In fact, Barry had lost George Sr.'s will.'
jokes.setup: Barry assures the family the will is at his office in a specific location.
jokes.punchline: The narrator immediately contradicts him — he'd actually lost the will.
jokes.explanation: The 'frayed wires hot plate' detail is a nice character touch before the narrator pulls the rug. The narrator-as-fact-checker is one of AD's signature devices, and this is a solid deployment. Barry's oblivious specificity makes the reveal funnier.

jokes.text: Maybe we could get Ice to cater it or something — I don't know what I'm saying!
jokes.setup: The family is processing grief and discussing a wake.
jokes.punchline: Maeby, supposedly grieving, immediately thinks of how to use the wake to get her mother near Ice for the emancipation scheme. She borrows Lucille's 'I don't know what I'm saying' verbal tic as a cover.
jokes.explanation: Two callbacks in one: Maeby's scheming and Lucille's verbal tic. Maeby unconsciously or deliberately borrowing Lucille's cover phrase is a nice layered detail. Rewatch value because you see the scheming more clearly on second viewing.

jokes.text: Gob: 'This about getting into Poof? / I mean, how does that not get me a cover?'
jokes.setup: Gob has just offered to be buried alive in his father's place.
jokes.punchline: Michael immediately sees through Gob's selfless framing — and Gob doesn't even bother to deny it, just pivoting to 'how does that not get me a cover?'
jokes.explanation: The callback to Poof magazine pays off perfectly here. Gob's wounded '...I mean, how does that not get me a cover?' is exactly the right tone — not denial but genuine confusion about why this wouldn't work. The Poof callback gives the episode structural coherence.

jokes.text: The flashback to Buster's parakeet: it landed on housekeeper Rose's wig, flew away when she took out trash, and flew into a transformer.
jokes.setup: The family debates whether to tell Buster about his father's death, referencing 'the parakeet incident.'
jokes.punchline: The parakeet's death is an absurd escalating chain: wig → trash → transformer → electrical zap. The follow-up gag is that Buster destroyed the family kitchen thinking that's where Rosa (the housekeeper) lived.
jokes.explanation: The parakeet story is exceptional. It has a perfect comedic chain: wig + trash + transformer = electrical death for a bird. Then the follow-up — Buster destroying 'the kitchen' believing that's where Rosa lived — is a beautiful second beat that reveals Buster's profound childlike confusion. The visual of Buster's kitchen destruction (inferred from the narrator + sound effect) earns extra points. This is exactly the kind of dense, layered AD joke that rewards attention.

jokes.text: Buster comes home mid-planning because 'the army had half a day.'
jokes.setup: The family is scrambling to hide the truth about the wake from Buster.
jokes.punchline: Buster arrives unexpectedly because 'the army had half a day' — treating military service like a school day with an early dismissal.
jokes.explanation: Perfectly Buster. The phrase 'half a day' is so mundane and childlike — like a grade schooler — applied to military service. Nobody else in television could deliver this line. Economy is near-perfect: four words characterize Buster's entire relationship with reality.

jokes.text: Lucille tells Buster it's 'your father's birthday, which, coincidentally, is my birthday' — because they're twins.
jokes.setup: Buster asks if they're having a party; Lucille needs a quick cover story.
jokes.punchline: She claims it's George Sr.'s birthday — then adds 'which, coincidentally, is my birthday' — then has to be reminded that's because Oscar (George Sr.'s twin) is right there, and his birthday would match.
jokes.explanation: The joke works because of the awkward improvised lie — Oscar and George Sr. are twins, so of course sharing a birthday isn't a coincidence. Lucille's panicked misdirection is funny, and the Oscar addition adds a layer of absurdity.

jokes.text: Michael, upon hearing 'grandfather is dead,' asks: 'Are you okay? What? Is Ann telling everyone?'
jokes.setup: George Michael says 'I've got some bad news,' and Michael immediately panics about Ann.
jokes.punchline: Michael's first fear isn't family tragedy — it's that Ann is spreading news about George Michael's romantic life. He's far more worried about Ann than his father.
jokes.explanation: Excellent misdirection and character comedy combined. 'Is Ann telling everyone?' as a panic response to 'I've got bad news' is perfectly calibrated. Shows Michael's priorities are deeply personal — his fear of Ann's influence on his son exceeds any reaction to news of a death. The reveal (it's about Pop-Pop) recalibrates the scene beautifully.

jokes.text: It's your grandfather. Apparently, he's dead. / Are you okay? / Yeah. / I'm sorry, what were you saying about Ann?
jokes.setup: George Michael breaks the news of grandfather's death. Michael asks if George Michael is okay.
jokes.punchline: One beat of empathy, then Michael immediately pivots back to Ann — showing grandfather's death is less pressing to him than the break-up.
jokes.explanation: The structural reversal is clean: Michael asks about George Michael's feelings then immediately re-centers himself. 'I'm sorry, what were you saying about Ann?' is perfectly timed — the pause for grief is exactly one beat. Character-consistent for Michael's blind spots.

jokes.text: After hearing his grandfather is dead, Michael asks: 'Are you okay?' George Michael says yes. Michael: 'She wasn't for us. For those other guys.'
jokes.setup: George Michael has just told his father his grandfather is dead AND that he broke up with Ann.
jokes.punchline: Michael's 'she wasn't for us' treats his son's breakup as a shared victory, and speculating about 'those other guys' implies Ann is a low-value match — all while a death has just been announced.
jokes.explanation: Michael's 'she wasn't for us' is the most clingy, boundary-crossing dad reaction possible. 'For those other guys' implies Ann exists in a hierarchy of males who might date her — and she's at the bottom. The casual dismissal while a death sits in the room is nicely dark.

jokes.text: She wasn't for us. / No, she's a great girl. / For those other guys. / You think there are other guys?
jokes.setup: Michael is trying to be supportive about the break-up while clearly glad it happened.
jokes.punchline: 'She wasn't for us' (already funny for the collective 'us') is followed by a dismissive 'for those other guys' — which triggers George Michael's insecurity ('You think there are other guys?') showing he's not actually fine.
jokes.explanation: Two layers working simultaneously: Michael's possessive 'for us' reveals his unhealthy investment in his son's relationship, and 'for those other guys' accidentally triggers George Michael's jealousy, proving he's not actually over it. 'She wasn't for us' is one of the episode's most quotable lines — the collective pronoun is brilliantly wrong.

jokes.text: Gob explains the coffin trick to Buster: 'I'm gonna escape out the trap door... You close the trap door, bring in the pallbearers and bury an empty box. So it's a hoax.' — Buster: 'But why are you doing a coffin trick on Dad's birthday?'
jokes.setup: Gob explains the mechanics of being buried alive as a magic trick.
jokes.punchline: Buster, completely aware it's a 'hoax,' still asks why they're doing a coffin trick for a birthday — oblivious that it's actually a wake.
jokes.explanation: Buster's question is beautifully innocent — he correctly identifies the deception ('it's a hoax') but is still entirely fooled by the larger deception. This layered obliviousness is quintessential Buster.

jokes.text: Gob explains the coffin trick as 'black humor' — saying it's like telling your dad 'Look at you, you're a year older, a year closer to death.' Buster: 'Oh yeah, I guess that's kinda funny.'
jokes.setup: Buster asks why they'd do a coffin trick at a birthday.
jokes.punchline: Gob's explanation of 'black humor' — being a year closer to death — is tragically more accurate than he intends (George Sr. is supposedly dead), and Buster's uncertain 'I guess that's kinda funny' sells the moment.
jokes.explanation: The dramatic irony here is layered — Gob is using death as a metaphor for birthday humor not knowing it's actually a death wake, while the audience knows what's really happening. Buster's tentative acceptance ('I guess that's kinda funny') is a perfect beat.

jokes.text: Buster reveals he's not really in the army and needs a military uniform from Gob's stripper connections — leading to a debate about whether strippers wear clothes.
jokes.setup: Buster needs a military uniform to attend a family event without blowing his cover story about being in the army.
jokes.punchline: Gob has stripper connections. Buster is confused about whether strippers wear clothes at all, with Gob answering 'Not at the end of the show' and Buster earnestly asking 'You can wear stripper clothes when you're not stripping?'
jokes.explanation: Buster's genuine confusion about the mechanics of stripping is perfectly in character. The logic chain — fake army, stripper clothes, philosophical debate about stripping — is absurdist in the best AD sense. 'You can wear stripper clothes when you're not stripping?' is delivered with complete sincerity.

jokes.text: George Michael discovers George Sr. hiding in the lot next to the house. George Sr.: 'Hide me. Don't turn in Pop-Pop. Help Pop-Pop.'
jokes.setup: George Michael goes for a walk to grieve 'both losses' in private.
jokes.punchline: He discovers his supposedly dead grandfather alive, immediately asking to be hidden. The 'Pop-Pop' self-references are an adult man referring to himself in third person as a grandfather nickname — pathetic and funny.
jokes.explanation: The 'Help Pop-Pop' third-person plea from a fugitive patriarch is exactly the right register — pathetic, funny, and character-consistent. George Sr. manipulating his grandson immediately upon discovery is on-brand.

jokes.text: George Sr. reveals his perfect teeth: 'Years of brushing with Glisten.'
jokes.setup: George Michael is startled by his supposedly dead grandfather's appearance.
jokes.punchline: George Sr. reveals his teeth — and attributes them to Glisten — the very toothpaste the family was just sentimentally mourning him over.
jokes.explanation: This is a textbook AD callback. The family was just mourning George Sr.'s 'perfect teeth' and his Glisten obsession — now he's alive, showing off those very teeth, attributing them to Glisten. The structural payoff is nearly perfect: plants a comedic detail, builds emotional (faux-grief) context, then the callback confirms and subverts both simultaneously. Near-excellent joke.

jokes.text: Flashback: The Mexican authorities' attempt to fake George Sr.'s funeral is 'a less-than-perfect deception' — shown with chaotic crowd noise and shouting.
jokes.setup: Narrator explains the Mexican authorities faked a funeral.
jokes.punchline: 'Less-than-perfect deception' — with audio chaos suggesting a completely botched fake funeral.
jokes.explanation: A solid background gag. The understatement of 'less-than-perfect' for what sounds like complete chaos is in line with the narrator's dry style. Not exceptional but competent.

jokes.text: George Sr. sees a newspaper story about his wife giving 'mouth-to-mouth to a homeless man' — but the narrator reveals he knew the truth: she was kissing his brother.
jokes.setup: George Sr. explains why he came back: he saw a photo of Lucille in the local Mexican paper.
jokes.punchline: The paper described it as mouth-to-mouth on a homeless man; he knew it was his brother Oscar she was kissing (who presumably looks like a homeless man).
jokes.explanation: Layered joke: Oscar is indistinguishable from a homeless man in appearance (established earlier with the butcher shop insult). The Mexican paper reporting it as CPR on a vagrant while it's actually an illicit kiss between twins is wonderfully specific and dark. Rewards attention.

jokes.text: George Michael, comparing his breakup to George Sr.'s marital issues: 'Well, I understand what that feels like. I was dumped today.'
jokes.setup: George Sr. is lamenting his wife kissing his brother.
jokes.punchline: George Michael equates a teenage breakup with a wife's affair with her husband's twin brother — complete moral equivalence.
jokes.explanation: George Michael's self-absorption here is well-calibrated for a teenager — the earnest equivalence between a first breakup and a decades-long marital betrayal is both funny and poignant. Delivered without irony, which makes it work.

jokes.text: George Sr.: 'He wouldn't be wrong.' (in response to George Michael saying Michael would think he should be upset about George Sr. rather than Ann).
jokes.setup: George Michael says he can't tell his dad how upset he is about the breakup because Michael will prioritize grieving George Sr.
jokes.punchline: George Sr.'s blunt 'He wouldn't be wrong' — confirming that yes, his death should take priority over a teenage breakup.
jokes.explanation: Perfect economy. Three words from a supposedly dead man pointing out he's a bigger deal than a breakup. George Sr.'s casual self-importance is fully intact even while hiding in a lot.

jokes.text: George Sr.: 'Listen, if you pass a mini-mart, Pop-Pop gets a treat?'
jokes.setup: George Sr. and George Michael have just agreed to keep each other's secrets.
jokes.punchline: George Sr. immediately pivots from this heartfelt moment to requesting a convenience store treat — using the third-person 'Pop-Pop gets a treat?' like a toddler.
jokes.explanation: Exceptional economy and character integration. The third-person 'Pop-Pop gets a treat?' is childlike, undignified, and perfectly captures George Sr.'s id. A fugitive patriarch begging his teenage grandson for a convenience store snack using baby talk is a beautifully constructed comedic image. Highly quotable line.

jokes.text: George Michael tells Michael his 'grief is taking the form' of eating his grandfather's favorite foods in his room.
jokes.setup: Michael catches George Michael suspiciously sneaking food upstairs.
jokes.punchline: George Michael's on-the-spot cover story — 'eating grandfather's favorite foods' as a form of grief — is obviously a lie but Michael buys it.
jokes.explanation: Solid moment of George Michael's adolescent improvised lying. 'The form that my grief is taking' is a nicely pompous phrase for a 15-year-old covering for a fugitive. Works because it's just barely plausible enough.

jokes.text: Michael thinks George Michael is hiding Ann in the attic — 'From who, the Nazis?'
jokes.setup: Michael suspects his son is sneaking food to Ann hidden in the attic.
jokes.punchline: Tobias's 'From who, the Nazis?' — a dark historical reference applied to the absurd situation of hiding a mild-mannered girlfriend in an attic.
jokes.explanation: The Anne Frank gag — hiding Ann in the attic from unspecified persecutors — is dark but structurally smart. 'From who, the Nazis?' cuts right to the absurdity while making an uncomfortable historical joke that lands because of its brevity and the bizarre accuracy. Economy is exceptional.

jokes.text: Tobias cries about how amazing George Sr. was; Michael says 'That was actually an example of how not so amazing he was,' referring to something George Sr. did.
jokes.setup: Tobias is overcome with grief at a memory of George Sr.
jokes.punchline: Whatever memory Tobias is reliving, Michael clarifies it was actually an example of George Sr.'s failings.
jokes.explanation: Dry puncturing of grief sentimentality. Michael's flat correction of Tobias's fond memory — 'that was actually an example of how not so amazing he was' — is perfectly delivered deadpan. The show's central theme (George Sr. was terrible) compressed into one line.

jokes.text: Tobias confesses: 'All those years when I pretended to cry, I used to use Dad's death to get me going. I tried it with Mom's, but I'd just end up smiling and ruining it.'
jokes.setup: Tobias is experiencing what he describes as genuine grief for the first time.
jokes.punchline: He reveals his acting trick was imagining parents' deaths — but imagining Lucille's death made him happy, which ruined his fake crying.
jokes.explanation: This is the standout joke of the episode. It does three things simultaneously: (1) confirms Tobias is a bad actor who has to fake emotions, (2) reveals he used parental death as his emotional trigger — a dark but recognizable acting technique, (3) the specific detail that imagining Lucille's death made him smile and 'ruined it' is a devastating indictment of Lucille packaged as a confession about acting technique. The joke is character-perfect (Tobias as failed actor + Lucille as universally despised), structurally elegant (the two-beat confession), and deeply quotable. This is 8+ territory — comparable to the Snip Snap anchor in character integration and dark specificity.

jokes.text: Tobias's 'Here he comes. Here comes John Wayne. I'm not gonna cry about my pa. I'm gonna build an airport, put my name on it.' — then: 'Why, Michael? So you can fly away from your feelings?'
jokes.setup: Michael is refusing to grieve properly, prompting Tobias to do a John Wayne impression.
jokes.punchline: Tobias's 'So you can fly away from your feelings?' turns his own (bad) John Wayne impression into a grief counseling metaphor — delivered earnestly.
jokes.explanation: Tobias doing an unsolicited John Wayne impression while delivering therapy is peak Tobias — the failed actor and amateur therapist combined. 'So you can fly away from your feelings?' as the punch to the airport metaphor is a clever pivot. Great character integration.

jokes.text: Michael's grief breaks out not through tears but through: 'Hey, where the [bleep] are my hard-boiled eggs?!'
jokes.setup: Tobias has just predicted that Michael's bottled feelings 'will come out in the most unexpected ways.'
jokes.punchline: Michael's repressed emotion erupts not in tears but in fury over missing hard-boiled eggs — which George Michael has been sneaking to George Sr. upstairs.
jokes.explanation: This is the best joke in the episode and one of the show's great structural payoffs. Tobias literally predicts the form Michael's grief will take — 'in the most unexpected ways' — then the edit CUT immediately delivers the punchline: Michael screaming about missing hard-boiled eggs. The joke operates on four levels: (1) perfect timing/structure with Tobias's setup, (2) the eggs are missing because George Michael is feeding the supposedly-dead grandfather, (3) this IS Michael's grief breaking out in an unexpected way — just not emotionally, (4) the bleep makes Michael's profanity funnier. This approaches the Snip Snap anchor in structural elegance and payoff satisfaction. Likely the most rewatchable moment in the episode.

jokes.text: Narrator reveals the laborer digging Gob's burial hole had been 'hired by George Sr. three days earlier for the same hole.'
jokes.setup: Gob is impressed by how fast the hole was dug.
jokes.punchline: George Sr. had already ordered the same hole dug, presumably for his own purposes — suggesting either escape tunnels, hiding contraband, or something equally shady.
jokes.explanation: The joke is efficient: George Sr. was planning his own escape or burial-related scheme, so the grave Gob needs already exists. This kind of layered retroactive plotting is AD's signature. Works especially well on rewatch when you understand the full context of George Sr.'s schemes.

jokes.text: Buster finds a birthday card: 'Someone wanted to send you a birthday greeting. See you soon!' and calls it 'wicked.'
jokes.setup: Buster is looking for a birthday card for the event he thinks is a birthday party.
jokes.punchline: The card 'See you soon!' is darkly appropriate for someone supposedly dead — Buster is delighted by it without understanding the irony.
jokes.explanation: Buster calling a card that says 'See you soon!' — for a dead person — 'wicked' is a nice dark irony moment. His obliviousness makes it land better than any knowing joke about death would.

jokes.text: My father's dead, Maeby. / Yeah, but you're not, and Ice is right there.
jokes.setup: Lindsay is wearing funeral-appropriate frumpy clothes. Maeby tells her to dress up.
jokes.punchline: Lindsay uses her father's death as an excuse to dress down. Maeby's response — 'Yeah, but you're not, and Ice is right there' — is a completely amoral reframe that puts vanity over grief.
jokes.explanation: 'Yeah, but you're not' is a perfectly economical dismissal of grief as an excuse for unattractiveness. Maeby's complete moral inversion (the living have no excuse to not look hot) is character-perfect and darkly funny.

jokes.text: Maeby tells Lindsay that 'All Pop-Pop ever wanted was to see you with another man besides Daddy' — Lindsay: 'You're right. I'm gonna throw on a skirt, take off my underwear and make your Pop-Pop proud.'
jokes.setup: Maeby manipulates Lindsay into pursuing Ice at the wake.
jokes.punchline: Lindsay's version of honoring her father's memory involves going commando at his wake — enthusiastically agreeing that this is what Pop-Pop would have wanted.
jokes.explanation: Lindsay deciding to remove her underwear to honor her father's memory at his wake is wonderfully dark. Her enthusiastic 'make your Pop-Pop proud' frames her vanity/lust as filial devotion. The line is highly quotable and very Lindsay — she finds a way to make everything about herself and her desires.

jokes.text: Barry (looking at Lindsay): 'Let me ask Barry. That might be enough right there.'
jokes.setup: Tobias is asking whether Lindsay's flirtatious behavior counts as an 'affair' for emancipation purposes.
jokes.punchline: Barry's implication that simply looking at Lindsay might constitute enough for emancipation — suggesting her behavior is already sufficiently scandalous.
jokes.explanation: Functional joke about Lindsay's scandalous appearance at a wake. The Barry reaction is mildly funny but not particularly sharp. Below average for this episode.

jokes.text: Lucille on Oscar wearing George Sr.'s suit: 'Tell my brother you don't wear a dead man's pants. Shame on him. And you say that to him. Say it to me like you're gonna say it to him.' / 'I'm probably not gonna say it to him.'
jokes.setup: Lucille is furious Oscar is wearing George Sr.'s suit.
jokes.punchline: Michael's 'I'm probably not gonna say it to him' after being forced to rehearse the confrontation.
jokes.explanation: The 'say it to me like you're gonna say it to him' is a great escalation of Lucille's controlling behavior — she wants a rehearsal of Michael's confrontation. His deadpan 'I'm probably not gonna say it to him' is perfect deflation. Good structure.

jokes.text: Michael shows Gob Ann, the girl who 'ripped his heart out, whose face will always be etched in my mind' — cut to Ann's unremarkable face.
jokes.setup: Michael describes Ann with great dramatic intensity as the girl who broke George Michael's heart.
jokes.punchline: Gob sees her — 'Her?' — implying Ann is so plain that the passionate description is hilariously disproportionate.
jokes.explanation: 'Her?' is one of the most quotable moments in the series. The setup — dramatic romantic language about a girl — followed by the single syllable 'Her?' perfectly encapsulates the show's running gag about Ann's unremarkableness. The economy is exceptional. A comedy touchstone for the series.

jokes.text: Michael invites Ann to the wake: 'It's as Ann as the nose on Plain's face.'
jokes.setup: Michael is trying to explain that it was obvious George Michael and Ann cared for each other.
jokes.punchline: 'As plain as the nose on your face' becomes 'As Ann as the nose on Plain's face' — a malapropism that simultaneously mixes up the idiom AND makes a pun on Ann's plainness.
jokes.explanation: This is one of the most ingeniously constructed jokes in the episode — possibly the most technically impressive. 'As Ann as the nose on Plain's face' does something nearly impossible: it's a malapropism that IMPROVES on the original idiom by making it a pun. 'Plain' serves as a surname replacement for 'your' while also meaning 'plain' as in plain-looking (Ann's established characteristic). The joke requires knowing the original idiom AND the running Ann-is-plain gag to fully land. This is the kind of wordplay that AD is celebrated for. Rivals the hard-boiled eggs moment as the episode's peak.

jokes.text: Buster arrives at the wake in a military outfit Gob got from strippers — 'All regulation, Michael. To get the top right, the pants had to be a little snug.'
jokes.setup: Buster needed a military uniform from Gob's stripper connections.
jokes.punchline: The outfit is clearly stripper-caliber — tight pants explained away as 'regulation' — and Buster is completely unaware of the incongruity.
jokes.explanation: Callback to the earlier stripper-clothes conversation. Buster's earnest defense of 'all regulation' while wearing what is clearly stripper-military wear is very funny. 'The pants had to be a little snug' as the only concession is perfect understatement.

jokes.text: Michael remembers what Buster did to 'what he thought was Rose's car with what he thought was Rose's favorite toy.' — Cut to: driver being attacked.
jokes.setup: Michael recalls the parakeet incident flashback to contextualize why Buster can't find out the truth.
jokes.punchline: A brief flash implies Buster attacked someone's car/person thinking it was related to Rose — the specificity of 'what he thought was Rose's favorite toy' suggests an intensely embarrassing/violent misidentification.
jokes.explanation: Second callback to the Buster/parakeet incident. The 'what he thought was' construction does a lot of work — implying multiple levels of Buster's misidentification. The implication of violence is dark and effective.

jokes.text: Anyone. / Anyone? / Perhaps my son George Michael would like to say something.
jokes.setup: Michael invites anyone to say something at the wake. Silence falls.
jokes.punchline: The silence extends through an 'Anyone?' and then Michael specifically calls on his own son — implying the entire family refused to speak voluntarily at their father/grandfather's wake.
jokes.explanation: The awkward silence is a staple of the show, and it works here as a reflection of the family's dysfunction — none of them want to speak sincerely about George Sr. The 'Anyone? Anyone?' echo is slightly Ferris Bueller-ish but works in context.

jokes.text: George Michael's eulogy devolves from emotional tribute into taking a catering order: '...I'll be bringing you some salmon rolls right away... in... heaven.'
jokes.setup: George Michael is delivering what seems like a heartfelt eulogy about his grandfather.
jokes.punchline: He seamlessly pivots mid-eulogy into a catering order for Ice, ending with 'in... heaven.'
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly constructed. The eulogy setup builds genuine emotional expectation, then the mid-sentence pivot to a catering order is completely unexpected. 'I'll be bringing you some salmon rolls right away... in... heaven' — the pause before 'in heaven' showing George Michael trying to salvage it — is perfect. Then Ice's callback to the chicken wings ('How many times I gotta tell this kid?') makes it a self-contained comedic moment. One of the episode's best.

jokes.text: Ice: 'How many times I gotta tell this kid — chicken wings!'
jokes.setup: George Michael's eulogy ends with him offering 'salmon rolls' to George Sr. in heaven.
jokes.punchline: Ice's exasperated 'chicken wings!' — revealing he's been correcting George Michael's catering orders throughout the wake.
jokes.explanation: Perfect button to the eulogy joke. 'How many times I gotta tell this kid?' implies an ongoing battle over catering orders that we haven't seen, which is funnier than if we had. Tight economy.

jokes.text: Lindsay's eulogy: Maeby whispers 'Wipe, Mom. Give it a wipe.' / 'That's a home run.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay gets up to speak at the wake while dressed to impress Ice.
jokes.punchline: Maeby is coaching Lindsay's appearance for flirting purposes mid-eulogy, treating the speech as an opportunity to attract Ice rather than honor the dead.
jokes.explanation: Maeby directing her mother's wake behavior like a flirting coach — 'give it a wipe' for what is presumably a wardrobe adjustment and 'that's a home run' — is wonderfully mercenary. The reversal of parent/child roles is perfectly executed. Maeby is a better operator than anyone in the family.

jokes.text: Michael's eulogy is interrupted by a blender: 'Ice, can we just cool it on the smoothies for right now?' Family: 'There are smoothies?'
jokes.setup: Michael begins his heartfelt eulogy.
jokes.punchline: Ice is blending smoothies at a wake; Michael has to pause his eulogy to ask him to stop; the family perks up at the mention of smoothies.
jokes.explanation: Ice blending smoothies mid-eulogy perfectly characterizes him — he's catering, not grieving. 'There are smoothies?' from a family member (probably Lucille or Buster) is a nice button showing the family is equally focused on refreshments over grief.

jokes.text: Gob asks Michael to move the eulogy outside 'so I can time being lowered into Dad's grave with your big finish.'
jokes.setup: Michael has just delivered what he thinks is a sincere, meaningful eulogy.
jokes.punchline: Gob immediately tries to co-opt the emotional climax for his burial-alive illusion — treating Michael's grief as staging for his magic trick.
jokes.explanation: Gob's request to sync his magic stunt with Michael's 'big finish' epitomizes his total inability to read emotional situations. Character integration is exceptional — this is completely Gob. 'Your big finish' treating a sincere eulogy as a performance is perfectly oblivious.

jokes.text: George Michael says 'I have Pop-Pop in the attic.' Michael interprets this as: 'The mere fact that you call making love 'pop-pop' tells me you're not ready.'
jokes.setup: George Michael, trying to confess about hiding his grandfather, says 'I have Pop-Pop in the attic.'
jokes.punchline: Michael completely misreads it as a euphemism for sex, lecturing his son about sexual readiness.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's top-tier jokes. The double meaning of 'Pop-Pop' — grandfather's nickname AND Michael's misread as a sex euphemism — is perfectly constructed. 'The mere fact that you call making love pop-pop tells me you're not ready' is a complete, self-contained, deeply character-specific joke: Michael's father-son boundary issues, his anxiety about George Michael's relationship with Ann, and the nickname's ambiguity all collide at once. Highly quotable and rewatchable. Structurally close to the 8.7 level.

jokes.text: Gob's outdoor eulogy speech: 'The speeches we heard today are nothing more than words. But I will prove that I love my father more than anybody.' — then gets into the coffin.
jokes.setup: Gob takes over the wake to perform his burial illusion.
jokes.punchline: Gob's proof of love for his father is... a magic trick he conceived to get a magazine cover. The grandiosity of 'prove that I love my father more than anybody' preceding a staged burial stunt is exquisite Gob delusion.
jokes.explanation: Gob's narcissism framed as love is perfectly in character. 'Nothing more than words' followed by his own dramatic words is a nice self-undermining irony.

jokes.text: Hey! Before we lose the sun! Come on. / The speeches we heard today are nothing more than words.
jokes.setup: The family is processing Michael's emotional moment with George Michael.
jokes.punchline: Gob interrupts to begin his illusion with a hilariously theatrical opening line ('The speeches we heard today are nothing more than words') — which is both pompous nonsense and technically true.
jokes.explanation: 'Nothing more than words' is a great piece of accidental profundity — the tautology is perfect for Gob's self-important style. Sets up the illusion sequence well.

jokes.text: I will become my dead father's body... as I am lowered into his grave. / All right. Spin it back around. / Dead father? / Is he de — Dad's dead?
jokes.setup: Gob, mid-illusion, reveals to Buster (who thinks it's a birthday party) that this is about their 'dead father.'
jokes.punchline: Buster has been cheerfully participating in what he thinks is a birthday coffin trick and only now, mid-performance, realizes what's happening. His escalating confusion ('Dead father? Is he de — Dad's dead?') is the payoff of the entire Buster-doesn't-know subplot.
jokes.explanation: This is the structural payoff of a subplot that has been running for over half the episode. The moment Buster processes the information — mid-coffin-spin at a funeral — is perfectly timed. His processing stutter ('Is he de — Dad's dead?') captures someone whose brain can't process information this way. The fact that this revelation comes from Gob, mid-magic-trick, is peak Arrested Development.

jokes.text: Oh, my God! The card! The card! [Spectators Gasping] / Something's gone wrong.
jokes.setup: Buster, now realizing his father is dead, panics and presumably drops the coffin or does something catastrophic with it. He's clutching the birthday card he selected earlier.
jokes.punchline: Buster's panic is about 'the card' — the 'See you soon!' birthday card — which he now realizes is devastatingly appropriate. Gob's flat 'Something's gone wrong' is a masterpiece of understatement about whatever physical disaster has just occurred.
jokes.explanation: Callbacks firing simultaneously: the birthday card comes back as an unintentional dark joke, and Gob's 'Something's gone wrong' is absurdly calm given what's presumably a coffin disaster. The double callback (card + parakeet-precedent for Buster's fragility) earns high rewatch score. Gob stating the obvious as understatement is a reliable and well-deployed gag.

jokes.text: Michael spots Glisten toothpaste in the dirt and suddenly realizes George Michael's 'Pop-Pop in the attic' meant the actual George Sr.
jokes.setup: Michael sees the Glisten toothpaste tube in the dirt near the grave site.
jokes.punchline: The Glisten callback triggers Michael's realization — connecting: family's sentimental memory of George Sr.'s Glisten obsession → George Sr. hiding in the attic → now proven by the Glisten tube in the dirt.
jokes.explanation: A masterclass in planted-callback comedy. Glisten was introduced as a grief memory, confirmed by George Sr. himself in the attic ('years of brushing'), and now serves as the revelation device that unlocks the plot. The narrative use of a comedic detail as a plot pivot is exactly what makes AD exceptional. Structure score high because it's been building across the entire episode.

jokes.text: Michael noticed something in the dirt. [Sighs] Glisten. / And suddenly he realized what his son meant when he said he had Pop-Pop in the attic.
jokes.setup: In the chaos of the funeral disaster, Michael notices a toothpaste tube in the dirt.
jokes.punchline: The Glisten toothpaste (established as George Sr.'s obsessive brand) proves George Sr. has been on the property. Michael's 'Sighs' before saying the brand name is the comedic beat — the realization lands like a detective moment but triggered by toothpaste.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best callback payoffs. The Glisten toothpaste has been planted three times (the eulogy, George Sr.'s reveal, and now) and its use as a detective clue is both absurd (toothpaste!) and perfectly logical within the show's internal world. The [Sighs] before 'Glisten' suggests the performance of a slow realization — it's a parody of a dramatic reveal triggered by the most mundane object possible. Structurally near-perfect.

jokes.text: Michael tells Ice: 'My mother has something to tell you.' Lucille: 'I find you very attractive.' Ice: 'She cleans up nice too. Trust me.' Then: 'I think I'm finally off the clock.'
jokes.setup: Michael needs to distract Ice while he deals with George Sr.
jokes.punchline: Ice reveals he finds Lucille attractive too — implying he's seen her in some other context ('she cleans up nice, trust me') — then immediately steps back when off the clock.
jokes.explanation: Ice's suggestion he's seen Lucille in a less cleaned-up state is mildly interesting ('trust me' implies prior contact), and his professional disengagement ('off the clock') is consistent with his character. Solid but not exceptional.

jokes.text: Buster: 'I'm out of here, right?' after Michael announces George Sr. is alive.
jokes.setup: Michael announces that George Sr. is alive and upstairs — the family scrambles.
jokes.punchline: Buster, who just found out his father is alive AND had a breakdown about it, immediately asks if he can leave.
jokes.explanation: Brief Buster beat — his emotional overwhelm leading to immediate flight instinct. Functional character comedy, not exceptional.

jokes.text: Barry: 'I cannot believe it! And I just found the will!' — holding it up triumphantly just as the crisis has completely moved on.
jokes.setup: The family has just discovered George Sr. is alive; everyone is chaotic.
jokes.punchline: Barry announces he found the will — the thing that was urgently needed earlier — at the exact moment it's completely irrelevant.
jokes.explanation: Nice callback closure — Barry finding the will at the exact wrong moment completes his storyline with perfect incompetent-lawyer irony. The triumphant 'I just found the will!' while everyone ignores him is very Barry.

jokes.text: Thanks, Mikey. / Yeah. Well, now it's our secret.
jokes.setup: Michael has let his father escape rather than turn him in, despite his earlier speech about honesty.
jokes.punchline: 'Now it's our secret' — immediately after Michael's eulogy about always being honest with his son. Michael has become his father.
jokes.explanation: A structurally satisfying dark irony: Michael's entire emotional arc in the episode is about breaking the cycle of Bluth family secrecy, and he ends by becoming exactly what he criticized. 'Now it's our secret' as the episode's emotional button is excellent writing. The irony is earned — the joke is the whole episode's thesis.

jokes.text: George Michael reveals Ann was 'so moved' by Michael's eulogy that they got back together — Michael: 'Ah! Oh, is that great.'
jokes.setup: Michael learns the Ann situation has resolved thanks to his eulogy.
jokes.punchline: Michael's 'Oh, is that great' — a performance of happiness about the reunion that reads as entirely hollow. He clearly doesn't think it's great at all.
jokes.explanation: Michael's performative enthusiasm for Ann's return is a solid ironic beat. The 'Oh, is that great' deadpan is very Michael. Functional, not exceptional.

jokes.text: Michael: 'Okay, let's go dig up your uncle.'
jokes.setup: The episode's emotional beats are wrapping up; Michael and George Michael have reconciled.
jokes.punchline: Michael's casual 'Okay, let's go dig up your uncle' — pivoting to the entirely absurd practical problem of Gob still being buried in the yard.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best lines and a quintessential AD joke. 'Okay, let's go dig up your uncle' delivered with total deadpan pragmatism after an emotional family reconciliation scene is perfect. It acknowledges the ongoing absurd crisis (Gob is literally buried) while treating it as a mundane errand. Economy is perfect — six words close the episode on exactly the right note. Extremely quotable, very rewatchable.

jokes.text: 'On the next Arrested Development': Buster finds out about the death of Captain Kangaroo.
jokes.setup: Tag sequence: 'Buster finds out about the death of Captain Kangaroo.'
jokes.punchline: Given what we know about Buster's reaction to his father's death, the implication is that news of Captain Kangaroo's death will provoke a similar or worse breakdown — Buster treating a childhood TV host's death as equivalent to a parent's.
jokes.explanation: Great tag. Buster's emotional fragility around parental figures (established throughout the episode) extended to a TV character is perfectly calibrated. The implication that Captain Kangaroo's death will be treated with the same weight as a father's is both dark and character-perfect.

jokes.text: 'On the next': Michael: 'No, Pop-Pop does not get a treat. I just brought you a [bleep] pizza.'
jokes.setup: Tag: Michael is now caring for his fugitive father in hiding.
jokes.punchline: George Sr. has been asking for treats (callback to 'Pop-Pop gets a treat?') and now Michael is exasperated — having brought pizza and refusing to call it a treat.
jokes.explanation: Excellent callback to the mini-mart treat request. Michael's exasperated 'No, Pop-Pop does not get a treat — I just brought you a [bleep] pizza' confirms the treat-requesting has become a running source of irritation, and the bleep makes Michael's profanity funnier. Michael calling George Sr. 'Pop-Pop' while denying him treats is also a nice inversion.

jokes.text: 'On the next': Gob finally makes the cover of Poof magazine.
jokes.setup: Tag: The narrator casually reports the resolution of Gob's Poof magazine ambition.
jokes.punchline: The episode's throughline — Gob wanting to be in Poof — pays off with a magazine cover, presumably for the burial stunt.
jokes.explanation: Perfect structural closure for the episode's A-plot comedic thread. The Poof magazine obsession opened the episode and closes it — and the implication that Gob actually succeeded (by getting accidentally buried at a fake wake) is a wonderfully absurd payoff. The callback earns its place.

tmdb_overview: Lucille hires a bounty hunter, Ice, to find George Sr., who may be closer than everyone thinks. Gob tries to pull off an elaborate illusion, Maeby tries to set Lindsay up with Ice, and Michael tries to mend George Michael's broken relationship.
insight: Funeral setting spawns 45 jokes in 45 minutes—character comedy sustains cringe without relying on plot.
